title: Long-term Effects of Modest Weight Loss in Type II Diabetic Patients
abstract:
Long-term Effects of Modest Weight
Loss in Type II Diabetic Patients
Rena R. Wing, PhD; Randi Koeske, PhD; Leonard H. Epstein, PhD; Mary Patricia Nowalk, MSHyg;
William Gooding, MS; Dorothy Becker, MBBCh
\s=b\Since most obese patients with type II diabetes are unable
to achieve ideal body weight, this study examined whether
more modest weight losses would provide a long-term benefit.
Type II diabetic patients (N =114) were treated in a behavioral
weight control program and followed up for one year. Weight
loss
was significantly
correlated
with
improvements
in
glycosylated hemoglobin values at posttreatment (r=
.55) and
one year (r=.51). Patients who lost more than 6.9 kg or had
more than 5% reduction in body weight had significant im-
provements in glycosylated hemoglobin values at one year,
while patients losing less weight had nonsignificant changes
and those gaining weight had significant worsening. Thus,
modest weight loss can have a long-term impact on glycemic
control. However, the improvement in glycemic control for a
given weight loss was greater initially than at one year, sug-
gesting that energy restriction, in addition to weight loss, may
contribute to initial improvement. Neither percent overweight
nor diabetes treatment affected weight loss.
(Arch Intern Med 1987;147:1749-1753)
"M"umerous studies have documented the short-term bene-
* * fits of weight reduction for patients with type II
diabetes. Weight loss reduces hyperglycernia and hyperin-
sulinemia,1,2 and in patients with severe hyperglycemia,
may lead to increases in insulin secretion.3 Weight loss has
also been shown to increase high-density lipoprotein choles¬
terol (HDL-C) levels4,6 in type II diabetic patients. Most of
these studies, however, have examined the relationship
between weight loss and metabolic control only during the
short-term phase of weight loss. The long-term effects of
weight loss on glycémie control remain less clear.
In addition, there is little information available on how
much weight loss is needed to produce a long-term improve¬
ment in blood glucose control. Studying nondiabetic and
hyperlipoproteinemic subjects, Olefsky et al6 found that
weight losses of 10 kg were sufficient to produce short-term
improvements in insulin sensitivity and serum lipid levels.
However, we are not aware of any studies that have ex¬
amined whether weight losses of this magnitude, if main¬
tained, will lead to long-term improvements in blood glu-
cose control in type II diabetic patients. Given the difficulty
in producing and maintaining weight losses,7 it would be
helpful to patients and physicians to know whether modest
weight losses will lead to long-term improvement in
glycémie control. The present study provides evidence that
modest weight losses (>6.8 kg) confer a long-term benefit
for type II diabetic patients.
SUBJECTS AND METHODS
Subjects in this study were 114 individuals with type II diabetes
who were treated in a weight control program and then followed up
for one year after the program. These subjects represent 91% ofthe
total sample who entered the weight control program. The 114
subjects (33 men and 81 women) ranged in age from 31 to 71 years
(mean±SD, 53.5±7.4 years). All subjects met the criteria spec¬
ified by the National Diabetes Data Group8 for type II diabetes and
were currently being treated with insulin (n = 21), oral hypo¬
glycémie medication (n = 68), diet only (n = 23), or insulin plus oral
medication (n = 2). Diabetes had been diagnosed for an average of
6.5 years (range, six months to 25 years) prior to entry into the
study. To be eligible for the program, subjects were required to be
20% or more above ideal weight for height; on the average, subjects
weighed 97.5 kg and were 59% above their ideal weight.
Weight Control Program
Subjects participated in one ofthree research studies investigat¬
ing behavioral strategies to improve weight loss in individuals with
type II diabetes.9"11 Although the specifics of the program varied
across studies and across conditions within a study, the basic
treatment format remained the same. Subjects were treated in
groups and seen frequently (usually weekly) for ten to 16 weeks,
monthly for the next six months, and then at nine and 12 months.
The 12-month follow-up meeting is the major focus of the present
study. Subjects were given individualized energy goals designed to
produce a 1-kg/wk weight loss and were taught to restrict their
energy intake using either an exchange system or an energy-
counting diet. The major emphasis of the diet was on restricting
the intake offat, but subjects were also encouraged to restrict their
intake of simple carbohydrates and to increase their intake of
complex carbohydrates and fiber. Three-day food records com¬
pleted by 48 subjects in the first study12 showed that on the average
subjects consumed 8551.2 J/d (2036 calories/d) at the start of
treatment, with 18% as protein, 40% as fat, and 40% as carbohy¬
drate. At the end ofthe treatment, subjects reported consuming an
average of 5880 J/d (1400 calories/d), with 21% as protein, 35% as
fat, and 44% as carbohydrates. All subjects were also encouraged
to increase their daily activity, particularly by increasing their
walking. Exercise goals were gradually increased over the course
of the program, with a final goal of walking 3.2 km/d, five days a
week.
Accepted for publication June 23, 1987.
From the University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine.
Reprint requests to Western Psychiatric Institute and Clinic, 3811O'Hara
St, Pittsburgh, PA 15213 (Dr Wing).
Downloaded From: http://archinte.jamanetwork.com/ by a Karolinska Institutet University Library User  on 06/01/2015
 *P<.001.
tP<05.
^Sample size for each measure varies due to missing data and subjects receiving insulin.
§P<,01.
»P-c.001.
tP<.05.
tP<.01.
Behavior modification strategies were used to promote long-
term weight loss. Subjects were taught to modify the cues in the
environment that lead to eating by purchasing controlled-energy
food, eating in one designated eating place, and slowing the act
of eating. Various reinforcers were used to support new eating
and exercise habits. Subjects were praised for positive changes
and taught to use self-reinforcement techniques. Although the
program was offered free of charge, patients were required to
deposit $85 at the start of the study and were refunded por¬
tions of the deposit for weight loss ($2 to $3 per 0.5 kg up to 9 kg),
portions for homework assignments, and portions for attending
follow-up sessions ($15 to $25 for attending six-month and one-year
follow-up).
As a result of weight loss, many subjects required changes in
their diabetes medication. During the initial ten- to 16-week
intervention, a three-hour, postprandial blood glucose measure¬
ment was obtained at each treatment meeting by trained staff
using a reflectance meter. The project physician used this informa¬
tion to adjust insulin or oral medication according to a standard
algorithm. During the year of follow-up, medication changes were
made by each subject's own physician.
Measurements
The following measurements were obtained at pretreatment and
one-year follow-up. Weight was measured in street clothes, with¬
out shoes, using a balance-beam scale. Percent overweight was
calculated by comparing the subject's weight with the ideal weight
for height specified on the Metropolitan Life Insurance norms
(Statistical Bulletin ofthe MetropolitanLife Insurance Company,
1959, vol 40, pp 2-3). Body mass index, which provides an estimate
of body fat,13 was calculated by formula (kilogram per square
meter).
Blood pressure was measured according to standard protocol
procedures." A normal adult or obese cuff was used. Three
measurements were taken, with at least a 30-s intermeasurement
interval. The mean of the last two measurements served as the
primary measurement ofblood pressure. The nurse was unaware of
the amount of the subject's weight loss.

tions of the deposit for weight loss ($2 to $3 per 0.5 kg up to 9 kg),
portions for homework assignments, and portions for attending
follow-up sessions ($15 to $25 for attending six-month and one-year

their diabetes medication. During the initial ten- to 16-week
intervention, a three-hour, postprandial blood glucose measure¬
ment was obtained at each treatment meeting by trained staff
using a reflectance meter. The project physician used this informa¬
tion to adjust insulin or oral medication according to a standard
algorithm. During the year of follow-up, medication changes were
made by each subject's own physician.
The following measurements were obtained at pretreatment and
one-year follow-up. Weight was measured in street clothes, with¬
out shoes, using a balance-beam scale. Percent overweight was
calculated by comparing the subject's weight with the ideal weight
for height specified on the Metropolitan Life Insurance norms
(Statistical Bulletin ofthe MetropolitanLife Insurance Company,
1959, vol 40, pp 2-3). Body mass index, which provides an estimate
of body fat,13 was calculated by formula (kilogram per square
Blood pressure was measured according to standard protocol
procedures." A normal adult or obese cuff was used. Three
measurements were taken, with at least a 30-s intermeasurement
interval. The mean of the last two measurements served as the
primary measurement ofblood pressure. The nurse was unaware of
the amount of the subject's weight loss.
Cholesterol and triglycéride levels were measured after an
overnight fast using enzymatic procedures; triglycéride levels
were measured on the glycerol obtained after hydrolysis with
lipase.1516 High-density lipoprotein cholesterol values were ana¬
lyzed by enzymatic procedures after heparin-manganese precipita¬
Fasting plasma blood glucose levels were analyzed with a glucose
analyzer. The fasting insulin value was analyzed by radioim-
munoassay.18 The HbAj value was measured on saline-incubated
samples by column chromatography using a water bath at approx¬
imately 22°C and low, medium, and high control samples.19 The
intra-assay coefficient of variation for hemoglobin A1 (HbA,) was
2% and the interassay coefficient of variation was 5% and was
similar for the high, medium, and low samples. The laboratory
normal value for HbA, is 6.1% ±0.6%.
Subjects lost an average of 5.6±4.0 kg (range, -17 to
+ 1.8 kg) at the end of treatment and maintained a loss of
- 56 to +7.7 kg) at one year. To assess
the long-term effects of specific amounts of weight loss on
glycémie control, patients were divided into five categories
according to their weight loss from pretreatment to one
year (gained; lost 0 to 2.3 kg; lost 2.4 to 6.8 kg; lost 6.9 to
13.6 kg; lost >13.6 kg). The number of patients in each
weight loss category and the average changes in glucose and
insulin levels experienced over the one-year follow-up are
Analyses of covariance were used to compare the changes
from pretreatment to one-year follow-up for patients in the
five weight loss categories, adjusting for pretreatment
levels. A log transformation was used to normalize the
distribution of insulin. There were significant differences
between the weight loss categories for changes in HbA!
(P<.001), fasting blood glucose (P<.001),
(P<.001) levels. F tests for simple effects were done to
determine which of the weight loss categories had signifi¬
cant changes over time. As seen in Table 1, patients who lost
more than 13.6 kg experienced significant improvement in
HbA!, fasting blood glucose, and insulin levels at one year.
These patients had an average weight loss of 27 kg but were
still 20% above ideal body weight at the end of the study. It
is important to note that patients with more modest weight
losses of 6.9 to 13.6 kg (mean weight loss of 9.9 kg) also
experienced significant improvement in all of these mea¬
surements. These positive results were obtained despite
the fact that these patients remained 42% above their ideal
body weight. The categories of patients losing less than
6.9 kg had nonsignificant changes in HbA, and fasting blood
glucose levels, while patients who gained weight had signif¬
icant worsening of glycémie control.
Pattern of changes in weight and hemoglobin A, (HbA,) levels for
subjects who lost more than 13.6 kg at one-year follow-up. Shaded
area shows HbA, level (mean ±2 SDs) of nondiabetic .subjects.
Similar significant effects (P<.001) of weight change on
HbA, values were seen when patients were divided into five
categories according to percent of body weight lost. Sub¬
jects who lost 5% to 10% of their body weight (P<.03) and
those losing greater than 10% oftheir body weight (P<.001)
had significant improvements in HbA, values (Table 2).
Changes in oral hypoglycémie medication and insulin
dose were also significantly related to weight loss category.
Reductions in medication were made for 100% of those who
started the study while receiving medication and lost
13.6 kg, compared with 63%, 46%, 40%, and 16% of those
losing 6.9 to 13.6 kg, 2.4 to 6.8 kg, 0 to 2.3 kg, or gaining
weight, respectively ( 2 [d/=4] = 12.9, P<.01).
To determine whether other coronary heart disease risk
factors improved with weight loss, analyses of covariance
were done on the lipid and blood pressure measures. A log
transformation was used to normalize the distribution of
triglycéride levels. Changes in triglycéride (P<.001) and
HDL-C (P<.001) levels differed significantly as a function
of weight loss category (Table 3). Those patients who lost
more than 13.6 kg and those who lost 6.9 to 13.6 kg both
experienced positive improvements in these lipid levels.
Changes in total cholesterol values and in systolic and
diastolic blood pressure did not differ significantly across
the five weight loss categories.
Weight losses were strongly associated with improve¬
ments in HbA, levels at both posttreatment (r=.55,
P<.001) and one year (r=.51, P<.001). The amount of
improvement in the HbA, level for a given weight loss,
however, was greater immediately after treatment than at
one-year follow-up. The regression equation expressing the
relationship between weight loss (in kilograms) and the
change in HbA, values from pretreatment to posttreatment
was as follows: HbA, change = 0.18 (weight change)+ 0.31.
change = 0.13 (weight change) + 0.51. Thus, immediately
after treatment, a weight loss of 5 kg was associated with a
reduction in the HbA, level of 0.6%, whereas at one year, a
weight loss of 5 kg was associated with only a 0.1% reduction
The difference between the initial and long-term effect of
weight loss can also be seen in the Figure. This graph
presents the pattern ofchanges in weight and HbA, level for
subjects who maintained a loss of more than 13.6 kg at one-
year follow-up. As seen in this Figure, subjects lost 14 kg
during the 12-week treatment and 14 kg more between week
12 and week 36. However, the majority of the change in
HbA, values occurred during the initial 12-week treatment
(-2.4%, P<.01) with nonsignificant changes in HbA,
values (-0.7%, not significant) during the subsequent six
months. Thus, the initial weight loss and change in energy
intake seem to have a more pronounced effect on HbA,
values than the subsequent changes. These data also sug¬
gest that maintenance of improved body weight can have a
long-term effect on glycémie control; subjects maintained
their weight losses between 36 and 64 weeks and HbA,
values showed only a slight, nonsignificant variation.
Analysis of variance techniques were used to determine
whether pretreatment characteristics affected the magni¬
tude of weight loss or the glycémie response to weight loss.
Table 4 shows the effect of patient's sex, diabetes treat¬
ment, and percent overweight on the changes in weight and
blood glucose levels from pretreatment to one year. The
difference in weight loss between men and women ap¬
proached significance (P<.06). The effect remained un¬
changed after adjusting for differences in pretreatment
weight but disappeared if the man who lost 56 kg was
omitted from the analysis. Neither diabetes treatment nor
percent overweight affected weight loss. Pretreatment
HbA, levels were significantly higher in women, in those
receiving insulin, and in those who were heavier at baseline.
Using either analysis of variance or an analysis of co-
variance to adjust for these pretreatment differences, there
was no evidence that these pretreatment variables had a
significant effect on changes in HbA, levels over time.
This study demonstrates that weight loss of greater than
13.6 kg results in significant improvements in HbA, levels
that are maintained at one year. Patients losing more than
13.6 kg achieved average HbA, levels of 7.1%, ie, 2 SDs
above normal control subjects, and all subjects could stop
taking oral hypoglycémie medication. Improvements in
insulin, triglycéride, and HDL-C levels were also observed
While weight losses of more than 13.6 kg produced
significant changes, only six (5%) of the 114 subjects
achieved this degree ofweight loss. The mean weight losses
obtained in the present study (5.4 kg at the end of the 12- to
16-week treatment and 4.5 kg at one-year follow-up), are
comparable to those reported for nondiabetic20 and diabetic7
patients in behavior modification programs and in other
forms of outpatient therapy. Thus, with our present weight
loss techniques, long-term results remain modest and the
important question is whether modest weight losses, if
a long-term impact on glycémie
The present study suggests that weight losses of 6.9 to
13.6 kg are also associated with significant decreases in
*Pretreatment differences were significant at P<.01.
tPretreatment differences were significant at P<.001.
HbA, levels that are maintained over the 12-month follow-
up. Significant improvements were also obtained for fasting
blood glucose, insulin, HDL-C, and triglycéride values.
These data indicate the importance ofmodest weight losses,
even if the patient does not achieve ideal weight. Moreover,
29% of our sample achieved this level of weight loss or
better. Similarly, subjects who lost 10% or more of their
body weight and those losing 5% to 10% oftheir body weight
showed significant long-term changes in HbA, levels.
Previous studies have examined patients immediately
following the short-term phase of weight loss and have
documented positive effects of modest weight loss in dia¬
betic patients.1"3 The present study suggests that these
positive effects extend through one year. It appears, how¬
ever, that the initial effects may be greater than the long-
term effects. For every 5 kg ofweight loss at week 12, there
was a 0.6% reduction in the HbA, level, whereas for every
5 kg of weight loss at one year, the reduction in the HbA,
level was only 0.1%. These data suggest that the initial
marked change in glycémie control may be due to a combina¬
tion of energy restriction and weight reduction per se. The
maintenance of the improvements in HbA, levels during
periods of stable body weight, however, suggests that long-
term improvements in body weight will have a long-term
effect on glycémie control. These findings are similar to
those obtained by Henry et al,21 who reported that the
majority (87%) of the fall in fasting plasma glucose levels
occurred within ten days of starting a controlled-energy
diet, although weight loss continued steadily throughout 40
days on the diet. With refeeding and weight maintenance,
there was an increase in fasting glucose levels, but plasma
glucose levels remained markedly below pretreatment
Data collected from a cohort of the subjects suggest that
patients changed not only their total energy intake, but also
the composition of the diet by decreasing fat intake and
increasing complex carbohydrates. These changes in diet
composition, independent of weight loss, or in addition to
weight loss, may have been related to the observed changes
The present study examined the variables associated
with weight loss. There was a tendency for men to lose more
weight than women, but this seemed to be due to one male
subject who lost more than 50 kg. Previous studies have
yielded inconsistent results regarding sex differences in
weight loss.22 The present study found no evidence that
diabetes treatment affected weight loss. Patients were not
randomly assigned to types of diabetes treatment, and
those receiving insulin had higher HbA, and fasting blood
glucose levels at pretreatment. Although these patients
receiving insulin appear to'have been in poorer control than
other patients, they were able to achieve the same weight
loss and improvements in HbA, levels. Other studies23,24
have also found only minimal differences in weight loss for
patients on diet only compared with those receiving either
In conclusion, these data suggest that modest weight
losses may help improve glycémie control in patients with
type II diabetes. Although an ideal goal would be sufficient
weight loss to normalize HbA, levels, with our present
weight loss techniques we do not accomplish this goal for
the majority of patients. Further research is needed to
determine whether decreases in HbA, levels of 1% to 2%
affect the patient's ultimate prognosis and to develop tech¬
niques that produce larger weight losses in diabetic pa¬
This investigation was supported by grant AM 29757-06 from the National
Institutes ofArthritis, Metabolism, and Digestive Diseases, Bethesda, Md.
